title: Writing Composition
objective: Students will explore writing composition concepts including Saying Sentences Before Writing Them, Writing Process Vocabulary, Simple Stories with Beginning and Ending and more.

steps.content: Let's explore Saying Sentences Before Writing Them! Have you ever wondered about this?
Say out loud what is going to be written; compose sentences orally before writing them down as preparation for independent writing
Time to try this out: Say a complete sentence aloud before attempting to write it
Before you starts writing a sentence, do they say it out loud first — testing how it sounds — before putting pen to paper?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Saying Sentences Before Writing Them: Say a complete sentence aloud before attempting to write it Rehearse a sentence orally more than once to hold it in memory
Today we're going to learn about Writing Process Vocabulary. Get ready for an adventure!

Know and use the vocabulary of the writing process — compose, plan, draft, revise, edit, proofread, genre, audience, purpose, narrative, recount, instruction, paragraph, sequence, and detail — and understand that these words describe distinct steps and decisions that all writers make, not just tasks to tick off
Let's practice together! Use 'compose', 'revise', and 'edit' correctly when describing the stages of writing

If you asked you to explain what they're doing when they write a story at school, could they walk you through each step using words like 'plan', 'draft', and 'revise' — showing they understand it's a process, not just one sitting?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Writing Process Vocabulary. Use 'compose', 'revise', and 'edit' correctly when describing the stages of writing Explain who the 'audience' is for a piece of writing and how that changes what they write

lessons.title: Simple Stories with Beginning and Ending

steps.content: Let's discover the amazing world of Simple Stories with Beginning and Ending!
Write simple narratives by sequencing sentences to describe events in order; narrate a single event or linked events with a beginning, middle, and ending or reaction
Let's practice together! Write a short story with at least three sequenced sentences
Can you write a short story about something that happened — with a clear beginning, middle, and end — so that someone who wasn't there could follow what happened and in what order?
Great job! Today we learned about Simple Stories with Beginning and Ending. Write a short story with at least three sequenced sentences Use time words like 'first', 'then', 'next' to order events
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Responding to Writing Feedback!

With teacher guidance, re-read own writing aloud to check it sounds right; listen and respond to questions and suggestions from teacher or peers to add detail, clarify meaning, and strengthen writing — this is the scaffolded beginning of writing self-evaluation, not an independent skill
Let me show you something cool. Reread own writing and spot a missing word or unclear sentence
When you's teacher asks them to read their own writing aloud and then asks "does that bit make sense?", can you listen to the feedback and make a simple change to improve it?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Responding to Writing Feedback. Reread own writing and spot a missing word or unclear sentence Add a detail in response to a question (e.g. 'Can you tell me more about...?')
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Writing to inform!
Compose informative or explanatory texts using drawing, dictating, or writing that name a topic and supply some information about it
Let's practice together! Write or dictate two or three facts about a familiar topic
Can you write a few sentences explaining something they know about — like how butterflies grow or how to make a sandwich — naming the topic and sharing some facts?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Writing to inform: Write or dictate two or three facts about a familiar topic Label diagrams or drawings with informative details
Let's discover the amazing world of Writing opinions!
Compose opinion pieces using drawing, dictating, or writing that name a topic or book and state a preference or opinion about it
Let's practice together! State an opinion clearly (e.g. 'My favourite animal is a dog because...')
Can you write a sentence or two giving their opinion about something — like their favourite book or food — and explain why they feel that way?
Great job! Today we learned about Writing opinions. State an opinion clearly (e.g. 'My favourite animal is a dog because...') Give at least one reason for an opinion
Today we're going to learn about Shared Research Projects. Get ready for an adventure!
Participate in shared research and writing projects; recall information from experiences or gather information from sources to answer a question
Let's practice together! Contribute ideas and information to a class writing project
When you is given a writing project that involves finding out about something — like an animal or a place — can they look up information and use what they find to write about the topic?
Great job! Today we learned about Shared Research Projects. Contribute ideas and information to a class writing project Draw or write facts learned from personal experience about a topic
Let's discover the amazing world of Sharing and Publishing Your Writing!
Read own writing aloud clearly enough to be heard by peers and the teacher; use digital tools to produce and publish writing
Let's practice together! Read own sentences aloud with clear voice and appropriate expression
Can you read their own writing aloud to the class clearly enough for everyone to hear and follow along — and have they ever typed or published a piece of writing using a computer or tablet?
Great job! Today we learned about Sharing and Publishing Your Writing. Read own sentences aloud with clear voice and appropriate expression Share a piece of writing by reading it to the class
